skill.name: ppt-editing-skill
skill.description: Edit existing PowerPoint files or templates with XML-safe workflows. Use for template-based deck updates: analyze layouts, map content to slides, duplicate/reorder/delete slides safely, edit slide XML in parallel, clean orphaned assets, and repack validated PPTX output.
skill.summary: Update PowerPoint presentations by directly editing slide XML files, enabling parallel editing and template-based workflows.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 75 patterns, but all are false positives. The 55 external_commands flags misidentified markdown code fence delimiters as Ruby backtick execution. The 4 path traversal flags misread XML formatting examples. The 14 weak crypto flags misidentified XML hex entity references. Real risks are limited to expected external command usage (cp, python, markitdown) and filesystem access for PPTX unpacking and repacking, both legitimate for this skill's purpose.

low_findings.title: External Command Execution
low_findings.description: Skill instructs agents to run shell commands (cp, python -m markitdown) and reference Python scripts (unpack.py, add_slide.py, clean.py, pack.py) for PPTX manipulation. Commands are hardcoded with no user input injection vectors.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Read and Write Access
low_findings.description: Skill reads user-provided PPTX files and writes to the working directory and /tmp/ for intermediate processing. File paths are user-provided but operations are limited to copy, extract, and repack PPTX archives.

content.value_statement: Manually editing PowerPoint files is slow and error-prone, especially for template-based workflows. This skill enables AI agents to safely modify PPTX files by directly editing slide XML, supporting parallel updates and consistent template adherence.
actual_capabilities: Extract and analyze PowerPoint templates using markitdown
Reorder, duplicate, and delete slides by editing presentation XML
Update slide content including text, images, and formatting
Clean orphaned assets and relationships from modified PPTX files
Repack and validate edited PowerPoint files for output
Preserve template layouts and design consistency across slides
limitations: Requires an existing PPTX file to work with, cannot create presentations from scratch
Relies on external Python scripts (unpack.py, add_slide.py, clean.py, pack.py) that must be available
Text replacement may cause layout overflow if new content exceeds placeholder dimensions
Complex visual elements like charts and SmartArt require manual XML editing expertise

use_cases.title: Corporate Template Updates
use_cases.description: Update branded presentation templates with new company information, team members, and quarterly data while preserving corporate design guidelines.

use_cases.title: Bulk Deck Customization
use_cases.description: Customize a master presentation for multiple audiences by duplicating slide decks and replacing content sections for each recipient group.

use_cases.title: Template Compliance Review
use_cases.description: Analyze existing presentations to ensure they follow organizational layout standards and identify slides that deviate from approved templates.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze a presentation template
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a PowerPoint template at /path/to/template.pptx. Analyze its slide layouts, available placeholders, and design patterns so I can plan content insertion.

prompt_templates.title: Update text content in slides
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to replace all placeholder text in template.pptx with new content for a quarterly business review. Preserve the existing slide order and formatting styles.

prompt_templates.title: Reorganize slide structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Reorder the slides in template.pptx so the executive summary comes first, remove slides 5 and 7, and duplicate slide 3 for a second use case example.

prompt_templates.title: Parallel multi-slide content replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a 20-slide template. Replace placeholder content across all slides with the attached data. Use subagents to edit each slide XML file in parallel, then repack and validate the output.

output_examples.input: A template.pptx with 10 placeholder slides and new content for a product launch deck
output: template.pptx preserved as the original unmodified copy
unpacked/ directory containing editable XML for all 10 slides
edited.pptx with all placeholders replaced by product launch content, validated and ready for delivery

output_examples.input: A slide with a 4-member team layout but only 3 team members to include
output: The fourth team member group (image and text boxes) removed entirely
No orphaned image references remain in the slide XML
Remaining 3 members retain original formatting and alignment

best_practices: Always copy the original file before editing so the source presentation remains unmodified
Use varied slide layouts to avoid monotonous, text-heavy presentations
Run clean.py after deleting slides to remove orphaned assets and broken relationships
anti_patterns: Never manually copy slide XML files, as this breaks relationship IDs and Content_Types references
Do not concatenate multiple list items into a single paragraph element in slide XML
Avoid clearing only text content without removing associated images or shapes from templates

faq.question: Can this skill create a PowerPoint from scratch?
faq.answer: No. This skill is designed for editing existing PPTX files or templates. You need a source presentation to work with.

faq.question: Why does the skill write to /tmp/ before the final path?
faq.answer: Python's zipfile module uses seek internally, which fails on some volume mounts such as Docker bind mounts. Writing to /tmp/ first avoids this issue.

faq.question: How are slide images handled during editing?
faq.answer: Images are preserved during unpacking and repacking. When removing content, delete entire element groups (image plus text boxes) to avoid orphaned references.

faq.question: Can multiple slides be edited at the same time?
faq.answer: Yes. Each slide is a separate XML file, so subagents can edit different slides in parallel after structural changes are complete.

faq.question: What happens to slide notes during editing?
faq.answer: The add_slide.py script handles notes references automatically. Notes are preserved during standard text editing operations.

faq.question: Why use defusedxml instead of the standard ElementTree?
faq.answer: The standard xml.etree.ElementTree corrupts PowerPoint XML namespaces. defusedxml.minidom preserves namespace integrity during parsing and editing.
